title: requests — entitlements
description: Entitlement keys this module reads at request time. Values seeded per Tenant by the entitlements module from Subscription plan + BusinessType.default_config. Enforcement points are the CreateAccessRequestAction (for pool-decrement) and the SPA (for feature-flag rendering).

kinds.description: Consumable pool decremented on submit. Resets on period roll.

entitlements.description: Whether the access-request flow is enabled at all for this tenant. Default TRUE on every plan tier — the substrate is universal. A tenant can opt out via ops-only setting for regulatory reasons (some regulated industries prefer a hard-only offboarding-then-onboarding flow, no in-band access requests).
Whether nightly approver reminders are enabled. Off for small tier, on for medium+.
entitlements.consumedOn: access-requests:send-reminders schedule entry (job is a no-op for tenants where this is false)

entitlements.description: Whether the approver can approve N requests in one action. Enterprise-only.
entitlements.consumedOn: POST /api/v1/access-requests/bulk-approve (route reserved; not implemented in Wave 1b)

entitlements.description: Max access requests a tenant can submit in a calendar month. Consumed on transition into `pending`. Rejected submissions (validation failures) do NOT consume. Default: 500 for small tier tenants; 5000 for medium; unlimited for enterprise. Guardrail against a runaway pattern (e.g. a bot spamming requests).
entitlements.consumedOn: AccessRequestSubmitted event (post-commit, so cancelled/rolled-back submissions don't consume)

notes: `access_requests.max_per_month` sits alongside the framework rate-limit (`throttle:access-requests` at 30/day per user). The pool is a TENANT budget; the throttle is a USER limit. They are complementary — the throttle prevents a single user from spamming; the pool prevents a whole tenant from consuming disproportionate approval-resolver compute.
Nothing here charges the tenant per request — access requests are a free substrate. Pool exhaustion is a safety-guardrail, not a billing lever.
